title: Nine Predictions For The Music Industry In 2026: How AI Reshapes Licensing And Power
text:
Business
Media
Nine Predictions For The Music Industry In 2026: How AI Reshapes Licensing And Power
By
Virginie Berger
,
Contributor.
Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.
Virginie Berger, based in L.A. covers music innovation, AI, and rights
Follow Author
Dec 29, 2025, 07:29am EST
CHINA - 2023/11/03: A smartphone displays the Spotify logo alongside artificial intelligence imagery, illustrating how AI is reshaping music distribution, licensing, and power dynamics. The image reflects the broader shift explored in this article, as AI-driven systems increasingly influence how music is monetized, valued, and governed. (Photo Illustration by Budrul Chukrut/SOPA Images/LightRocket via Getty Images)
SOPA Images/LightRocket via Getty Images
For most of the past decade, debates about technology and music have revolved around regulation, lawsuits, and moral positioning. That framing is no longer sufficient. By 2026, the most consequential shifts in the music industry will not be driven by courts or policy, but by markets responding to new economic realities created by AI.
AI has already altered
how music is created, distributed, and monetized
. What comes next is a deeper reset of licensing, valuation, and power. As institutions struggle to keep pace, platforms, private equity, and technology companies are quietly redefining how rights are priced, how risk is managed, and who gets paid.
The following ten predictions outline where the music industry is heading in 2026, based not on ideals, but on incentives. They track how AI pushes licensing downstream, how value concentrates or fractures, and how artists, publishers, platforms, and investors adapt when control over data, distribution, and attribution weakens.
Anna's Archive leak shifts disputes from permission to provenance
What already happened:
Anna's Archive scraped almost all of Spotify
in December 2025, including metadata for 256 million tracks and audio for 86 million songs, totaling nearly 300TB. The data is freely accessible to anyone, including AI companies.
The prediction:
By 2026, attribution and enforcement become practically impossible once large, consolidated datasets circulate openly. More AI models get trained on mixed, opaque datasets where origin can't be reconstructed with confidence. Licensing negotiations happen after deployment, framed as risk management rather than consent. Registration, reporting, and royalty fraud increases as behavioral and contextual data gets reused outside its original systems.
Why this changes everything:
The industry realizes control over training data has already slipped. Attribution, not access, becomes the real bottleneck. A growing divide emerges between companies investing in upstream attribution and inference-stage controls, and those betting on ambiguity and settlement later.
Institutional protection fails, market distinction becomes the only viable path
What already happened:
AI companies control capital, speed, and political alignment. Legal systems move slowly and are unlikely to produce a safe environment for creatives because that runs against capital interests. At the same time, automation is enabling mass production at a scale that rapidly devalues AI outputs.
MORE FOR YOU
The prediction:
By 2026, artists who retain leverage stop treating law and regulation as their main path forward. The arms race on efficiency is unwinnable. What remains is public preference.
Extreme efficiency leads to saturation. Saturation leads to devaluation. As AI output becomes abundant and interchangeable, its value drops in most contexts. That dynamic opens space for a narrower market where human-made work carries value because it is authored, intentional, and scarce.
AI will dominate uses where content simply needs to function. Human creators who survive will operate in contexts where identity, authorship, and intent still matter. This does not protect everyone. Many artists will be pushed out. Some will capture higher value per work precisely because they are no longer competing on volume.
What makes this possible:
Transparency. As long as AI use is disclosed, audiences will sort themselves.
An indie publisher consortium forms to collectively bargain with AI companies
What already happened:
IMPF publicly urged independent publishers
to reject AI licensing agreements
that fail to allocate at least 50 percent to songwriters and publishers.
The prediction:
Independent publishers form a collective bargaining structure to negotiate AI training and usage terms, effectively operating as a songwriter-focused credit union with real negotiating weight.
Why this happens:
AI licensing economics increasingly favor recordings over compositions, compress negotiation timelines, and magnify asymmetry. Acting alone becomes economically irrational. Collective leverage becomes the only way to demand parity and prevent publishing from being structurally underpriced in AI deals.
Large catalog owners turn AI training into a premium licensing business instead of acquiring more catalogs
What already happened:
Blackstone acquired Hipgnosis Songs Fund for $1.6 billion
in July 2024 and raised $1.47 billion through asset-backed securities in October 2024 for future activity.
The prediction:
These conditions make AI training licenses an attractive additional monetization path for large, well-cleared catalogs. Instead of relying solely on usage-based royalties, institutional owners can offer access to “clean” training data in exchange for upfront payments, priced around litigation avoidance and operational simplicity.
Why this makes private equity sense:
ABS covenants favor predictable cash flows. AI training licenses deliver immediate revenue without collection infrastructure, currency exposure, or streaming fraud risk. The catalog commands a premium precisely because it is positioned as cleared, defensible, and low-risk.
Universal Music Group–Udio AI music opt-in platform launches with limited artist participation
What already happened:
Universal Music Group and Udio
announced plans to launch an artist opt-in platform in 2026. The idea is to allow artists to voluntarily authorize the use of their recordings, voices, or stylistic elements for AI-related applications in exchange for compensation. Participation is positioned as optional and artist-led.
The prediction:
When the platform launches, adoption remains limited. Fewer than 100 artists with meaningful brand recognition or catalog value choose to participate.
Why participation stays low:
For most established artists, the financial upside does not outweigh the reputational and creative risk. Authorizing AI use of a voice or style introduces concerns around brand dilution, loss of control, and fan perception. Once those elements circulate in AI systems, they cannot be meaningfully recalled or constrained.
Artists who previously sold their catalogs often lack control over these decisions, while artists who retained ownership generally did so to preserve long-term value and identity. For them, short-term AI revenue offers little incentive to compromise that position. As a result, the platform attracts interest mainly from a narrow group of artists for whom visibility or experimentation matters more than brand protection.
Collecting societies owned by financial investors clash with songwriters over AI licensing
What already happened:
In the US, several major performing rights organizations that collect and distribute royalties to songwriters are now owned by private equity firms. This includes BMI, SESAC, and GMR. These organizations are increasingly under pressure to define how AI training, synthetic voices, and likeness rights should be licensed and monetized.
The prediction:
By 2026, at least one of these private equity–owned PROs adopts an AI licensing framework that prioritizes predictable, large-scale revenue over individual songwriter consent. That decision leads to a public conflict with creators, who push back against having their rights bundled into AI deals without clear opt-in, transparency, or control.
Why this is likely to happen:
Private equity ownership changes incentives. These firms focus on stable cash flows and scalable revenue models. AI licensing aligns well with that logic because it favors bulk agreements, standardized terms, and aggregated rights. Songwriters, on the other hand, care about consent, control, and how their work and identity are used. As AI licensing moves from theory into material revenue, the gap between those priorities becomes visible and eventually explodes into open disagreement.
Artists sue over unauthorized AI opt-in
What already happened:
Universal Music Group and Warner Music Group reached licensing agreements with Udio covering label- and publisher-controlled rights. UMG and Udio also announced plans for an artist opt-in platform, with rollout discussed for 2026.
The prediction:
In 2026, major artists who previously sold catalogs initiate lawsuits alleging breach of fiduciary duty after discovering voice or stylistic elements used in AI systems without explicit consent.
Why this becomes unavoidable:
Catalog sale agreements frequently include “all rights now known or hereafter devised.” Artists argue that voice and likeness rights remain separate from sound recording copyrights. The first case forces courts to clarify whether opt-in requires affirmative artist consent or whether consent can be inferred through label or publisher agreements.
AI optionality becomes a valuation variable for music catalogs
What already happened:
Pophouse is a Swedish investment company focused on acquiring and managing major music catalogs and artist brands with a long-term commercial strategy. In 2025,
Pophouse raised $1.2 billion
, with around $1 billion still available for acquisitions. The firm has been clear that it evaluates catalogs beyond current streaming income, looking at future forms of exploitation and expansion.
The prediction:
As AI-related uses move from theory into commercial reality, catalog buyers with long investment horizons begin factoring AI-related optionality into valuation models. This includes the potential to license, adapt, or extend catalog assets for AI-driven applications, even if no immediate deployment is planned.
Why this matters:
Once a major buyer formalizes this logic in a headline deal, it quickly becomes market practice. Sellers, advisors, and competing funds have to respond, because ignoring AI-related upside or exposure starts to look like mispricing rather than caution. Catalog valuations shift accordingly, even for deals that do not involve AI immediately.
AI detection systems introduce tiered royalty structures
What already happened:
Deezer launched AI detection
technology in June 2025 to filter fraudulent AI-generated tracks for royalty purposes with the ability to detect what is 100% AI generated.
The prediction:
In 2026, Spotify, Apple Music, and Amazon adopt similar AI detection systems—but go further than Deezer. They introduce three-tier royalty structures: (1) fully human-made  (2) AI-assisted content  (3) fully AI-generated content (minimal rates or zero).
Why platforms adopt this:
Without detection and classification, the entire royalty system breaks down. You can’t register works properly, license them correctly, or pay the right parties if you don’t know what the content is. As AI-generated volume explodes, platforms face operational chaos: PROs demand accurate metadata, labels demand proper attribution, publishers need to know what's a composition versus what's synthetic. Detection becomes infrastructure, not a policy choice. Tiered rates follow naturally once classification exists.
Taken together, these predictions point to a music industry that looks less protected and more exposed by 2026. Licensing does not disappear, but it changes character. It becomes less about permission granted in advance and more about risk priced after the fact. Value shifts toward actors who can offer scale, certainty, and insulation from legal and operational complexity.
For artists and rightsholders, this environment rewards clarity of positioning rather than reliance on institutional intervention. For platforms and investors, it creates opportunities to monetize saturation, authenticity, and compliance simultaneously. For everyone else, it raises uncomfortable questions about sustainability, enforcement, and fairness that markets will answer faster than regulators.
